title: podman system prune sometimes fails or podman stop doesn't wait for containers to stop
body:
### Issue Description

Sometimes when I run `podman system prune -a -f` immediately after stopping containers with `podman stop`, I get an error message and exit code 125.

If I run `podman system prune -a -f` again, I don't get the error.  So it seems like `podman stop` doesn't wait for containers to stop.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Start a number of containers like this:
 `for i in {1..10} ; do podman run -id --rm registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/tumbleweed ; done`
2. Stop them all and immediately prune them like this:
`podman ps -q | xargs -r podman stop ; echo STOPPED ; podman system prune -a -f ; echo $?`

Repeat the above steps until you get an error.

### Describe the results you received

```

Total reclaimed space: 0B
```

### Describe the results you expected

I expect that `podman stop` waits for containers to really stop so `podman system prune` doesn't exit with 125.

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

### Additional information

This bug was found thanks to openQA automation:
https://openqa.suse.de/tests/11383574/logfile?filename=serial_terminal.txt

comments.body: CC @dcermak

Thanks for filing the issue and the nice reproducer, @ricardobranco777!

Looks like a TOCTOU between listing and removing containers.  @dcermak, do you want to tackle it?

The race is not because podman stop did not stop them. podman stop does stop them and that triggers the podman container cleanup command in the background to clean up the resources used. And if you specify `--rm` that includes removing the container. If you want to make sure the container is removed by the time the command finishes use `podman rm -f` instead.
IMO the race must still be addressed in `system prune`.

Yes absolutely, the race can be trigger in many ways I assume.
My comment wasn't meant to say this report is invalid just that stop is stopping only not removing. Sorry I should have made this clear.
Thanks for highlighting that, @Luap99!
who is handling this?
Nobody yet.  I asked if @dcermak's interested above.
I can take a look at this @vrothberg if Dan hasn't already started
I only look at stuff that hasn't been touched in 30 days.

> I can take a look at this @vrothberg if Dan hasn't already started

It's all yours, @danishprakash.  Feel free to ping me if you have questions.

> I can take a look at this @vrothberg if Dan hasn't already started

Tracking in SUSE's Bugzilla: https://bugzilla.suse.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1212825
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@danishprakash any update?

> @danishprakash any update?

@rhatdan We've debugged the issue with @danishprakash for a few hours but so far haven't found the underlying issue yet.

@dcermak and I spent a couple of hours these past few weeks trying to debug this issue and here's what we have so far.

On face value, this is a data race which is reproducible when spanwing a bunch of containers and then stopping them and then immediately running the prune command. The prune command frequently complains of container(s) not found in database while removing them.

It seems that `podman stop` and `podman system prune` are in contention because of the `podman container cleanup` triggered by `podman stop` via `conmon` as part of the cleanup process. Here, `podman system prune` gets a list of containers to be cleaned up but by the time we get to the time-of-use, the `podman container cleanup` has removed the said container leading the other podman process(`system prune`) to error out. The rough timeline is shown below:

```
   podman stop                                                                         
   -------------
                                                                                       
          conmon                                                                       
          -----------------------                                                      
                                                                                       
                           podman container cleanup                                    
                           --------------------------------                            
                                                                                       
                 podman system prune -a -f                                             
                 ------------------------------------------
```

So far, we've tried to ascertain whether this is due to no proper locking in place on the databse which is causing the race. We explored this with boltdb and found that boltdb does indeed takes a file lock on the data file to prevent such race conditions[1].

We also ruled out the same with the new sqlite database backend -- We're still able to reproduce the issue but with a lower hit rate. Could be anecdotal at best.

Apart from locking at the db level, there are also multiprocessor locks held for individual containers, namely the (default)shared memory lock[2]. We followed the call trace for both the container cleanup and the prune operations to see if we might be operating without a lock or a lock held incorrectly but that doesn't seem to be the case.

Could you corroborate the above and perhaps even hint at what could be going wrong or if we're looking in the right direction? Thanks

#### Resources

- `podman container cleanup` command triggered via `conmon` post `podman stop`:
```
/usr/bin/podman --root /home/danishprakash/.local/share/containers/storage --runroot /run/user/1000/containers --log-level warning --cgroup-manager systemd --tmpdir /run/user/1000/libpod/tmp --network-config-dir  --network-backend netavark --volumepath /home/danishprakash/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes --db-backend boltdb --transient-store=false --runtime crun --storage-driver overlay --events-backend journald container cleanup --rm <cid>
```

- failure call stack w/ sqlite
```

- call stack w/ boltdb
```
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*BoltState).getContainerStateDB(0x7f37544dfa68?, {0xc00063dfc0, 0x40, 0x40}, 0xc00025bc00, 0xc0003a6b60?)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/boltdb_state_internal.go:416 +0x2c5
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*BoltState).UpdateContainer.func1(0xc0000540a0?)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/boltdb_state.go:832 +0x85
go.etcd.io/bbolt.(*DB).View(0xc000470150?, 0xc000757478)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/vendor/go.etcd.io/bbolt/db.go:893 +0x82
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*BoltState).UpdateContainer(0xc000470150, 0xc00025bc00)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/boltdb_state.go:820 +0x173
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*Container).syncContainer(0xc00025bc00)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/container_internal.go:334 +0x42
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*Container).State(0xc00025bc00)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/container.go:725 +0xa5
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*Runtime).PruneContainers.func1(0xc00025bc00)
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/runtime_ctr.go:1349 +0x45
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*Runtime).GetContainers(0x188f320?, 0x8?, {0xc000532020, 0x1, 0xc000500c00?})
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/runtime_ctr.go:1265 +0x1fb
github.com/containers/podman/v4/libpod.(*Runtime).PruneContainers(0x0?, {0x2a65108, 0x0, 0x0})
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/libpod/runtime_ctr.go:1363 +0xfd

        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/pkg/domain/infra/abi/containers.go:326 +0x1c5
github.com/containers/podman/v4/pkg/domain/infra/abi.(*ContainerEngine).SystemPrune(0xc000014f18, {0x1df6130, 0xc000134020}, {0x0?, 0x0?, 0xc0005a4300?, 0x0?})
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/pkg/domain/infra/abi/system.go:197 +0x485
github.com/containers/podman/v4/cmd/podman/system.prune(0x294e120?, {0xc000481640?, 0x0?, 0x2?})
        /home/danishprakash/code/go/src/github.com/danishprakash/podman/cmd/podman/system/prune.go:85 +0x2b5

[1] - https://github.com/boltdb/bolt/blob/master/README.md#opening-a-database
[2] - https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/lock/shm_lock_manager_linux.go

The race is expected, we first get a list of container than act on it. Like the other commands such as podman ps all that is needed from podman sytem prune to catch and ignore the `no such container` error.

The latter is what we also thought about but is there a way we can definitively differentiate between a legit `no such container`(i currently don't know if there is one) and one that arises in the current scenario? I guess since there's no external input to the prune command, this should work.

> The race is expected..

So we don't expect the in-memory container locks to prevent races between multiple podman processes?

> So we don't expect the in-memory container locks to prevent races between multiple podman processes?

Once a container is locked, no other process can acquire the lock of the container.  The general issue is a TOCTOU - at the time of using a container, it may have already been removed.

> So we don't expect the in-memory container locks to prevent races between multiple podman processes?

They do prevent races between processes, but this is a single container lock that prevents concurrent operations on a single container, i.e. things like start, rm, etc...

If you get a list of containers from the db they are always not locked. By the time you would use them they could already be removed. Every code path must handle this. For operations like ps, rm -fa, or system prune this just means ignore this error.

And it is important to handle it like this, if we try to use global locks that quickly makes podman super unresponsive.

On second thought this reasoning makes sense. So your advise would be that inside `PruneContainers`, we handle the `no container` error condition differently?
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0fb5b3acf05d984d6dd1c3a04a08b19c536597f1/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L1350

Handling this in just `PruneContainers` might solve for just one case but it would then leave out others e.g. `NetworkPrune`. I think this will require more changes than that, either we handle this in all of the prune code paths or in `syncContainer`.
In containersprune, I think we should only logerror if it is not NOEXISTS.

> On second thought this reasoning makes sense. So your advise would be that inside `PruneContainers`, we handle the `no container` error condition differently?
> 
> https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0fb5b3acf05d984d6dd1c3a04a08b19c536597f1/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L1350

The line should not cause this error, it is only logged there. I mean yes it is best to make that line ignore this error but it cannot be the error shown in the report above.
The error above is most likely thrown by the RemoveContainer() call
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0fb5b3acf05d984d6dd1c3a04a08b19c536597f1/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L1378-L1378

> > On second thought this reasoning makes sense. So your advise would be that inside `PruneContainers`, we handle the `no container` error condition differently?
> > https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0fb5b3acf05d984d6dd1c3a04a08b19c536597f1/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L1350
> 
> The line should not cause this error, it is only logged there. I mean yes it is best to make that line ignore this error but it cannot be the error shown in the report above. 

It is, we have captured multiple stack traces that point to this place (see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/19038#issuecomment-1663499304).

> The error above is most likely thrown by the RemoveContainer() call
> https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0fb5b3acf05d984d6dd1c3a04a08b19c536597f1/libpod/runtime_ctr.go#L1378-L1378

We did not see this function being called, the error happens earlier in `PruneContainers`. Getting the container state already fails, so podman never gets to trying to delete the container.
